Because this is a one-time grant rather than ongoing monthly assistance, your loved one can use the funds for immediate safety needs without affecting other benefits like Social Security or Medicare.

The program is administered through Colorado's Office on Aging, but you apply through your loved one's local county Department of Human Services. Each county processes applications differently, so processing times and availability may vary by location.
programs.savingsSource: Maximum grant amount from Colorado Office on Aging

otherRequirements: Colorado resident
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county Department of Human Services office, processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Find your county office
steps.description: Locate your loved one's county Department of Human Services office. Each of Colorado's 64 counties administers this program locally.

steps.title: Call to request an application
steps.description: Call your county office to request information about the Older Coloradans Cash Fund. Ask specifically about current availability and required documents.

steps.title: Submit required documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of age, income, residency, and citizenship status. Each county may have slightly different documentation requirements.

steps.title: Wait for review
steps.description: The county will review the application and determine eligibility. Processing times vary by county and current caseload.

applicationGuide.tip: Call your county office first to ask about current funding availability, some counties may have waiting periods when funds are exhausted.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID or driver's license
Proof of age (birth certificate, passport, or driver's license)
Proof of Colorado residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or mortgage statement)
Social Security award letter or most recent statement
Proof of all household income from last 30 days (pay stubs, pension statements, or tax return)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified non-citizen status (birth certificate, passport, or naturalization papers)
Documentation of specific need (estimates for home repairs, medical equipment prescriptions, or utility shut-off notices)

contacts.label: Colorado 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline to find your county Department of Human Services office

contacts.label: Colorado Department of Human Services
contacts.description: State office for general program information

applicationNotes: This is a one-time grant, your loved one cannot receive funds again in future years, even if circumstances change
Some counties may require estimates or quotes for home modifications before approving funds
Funding availability varies by county and fiscal year, some areas may have waiting periods when annual allocations are exhausted

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for this if they already receive Social Security or other benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes. Social Security, Medicare, and other federal benefits don't disqualify your loved one, but the income from those benefits does count toward the $2,400/month limit. Include all sources of income when calculating eligibility.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs more than $1,200 worth of home modifications?
faqs.answer: The program caps at $1,200 per person. Your loved one would need to cover additional costs through other programs, insurance, or personal funds. Ask the county office about other local assistance programs that might help with remaining costs.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you'll need proper authorization. Contact the county office to ask about their specific requirements for family members applying on behalf of an aging loved one. Some counties may require power of attorney or other legal documentation.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's county runs out of funding?
faqs.answer: Each county receives an annual allocation. If funds are exhausted, your loved one may need to wait until the next fiscal year or be placed on a waiting list. Call early in the fiscal year (July) for the best chance of receiving funds.

faqs.question: Can this money be used for anything other than home modifications and equipment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program also covers utility assistance, personal care services, respite care, and supplies related to aging in place. However, you cannot use funds for routine living expenses like groceries or rent.

faqs.question: How long does my loved one have to spend the money once approved?
faqs.answer: Each county sets its own timeframe for using approved funds. Ask your county office about their specific deadline, some may require purchases within 30-90 days of approval.

stateOverview.intro: Colorado offers 12 programs that can reduce costs and provide support for aging loved ones, from monthly cash assistance to free home energy upgrades. Nine of these are benefits requiring qualification (income limits, age, or care needs), while three are free resources available to most Colorado seniors. Programs range from Old Age Pension providing monthly cash to seniors 60+ with limited income, to PACE covering all medical and social care under one coordinated team. Start by checking the highest-impact programs first: those offering the most financial relief or comprehensive support for your loved one's biggest challenges.

startHere.name: Health First Colorado Buy-In (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Can save hundreds monthly on Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs for parents on limited income.

startHere.name: Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
startHere.why: Covers personal care and home modifications so your loved one can stay home instead of moving to a facility.

startHere.name: SNAP
startHere.why: Monthly grocery money that provides immediate budget relief for food costs.

startHere.name: Old Age Pension (OAP)
startHere.why: Monthly cash assistance specifically for Colorado residents 60+ with limited income and assets.

programs: Community Access Services (CAS) and Respite Care
byNeed.description: Personal care, home modifications, and coordinated support to avoid nursing home placement.
Regular monthly support for basic living costs, groceries, and medical expenses.
Free energy upgrades and grants for home repairs and safety equipment.
Expert help navigating Medicare and legal issues at no cost.
Paid job training placements for adults 55+ with low income.

quickFacts: Colorado's Old Age Pension provides state-specific cash assistance for residents 60+ beyond federal programs
Home Care Allowance offers up to $1,500/month specifically to help Colorado families pay for personal care and home modifications
Older Coloradans Cash Fund provides one-time grants up to $1,200 for immediate home safety needs
PACE operates in Colorado as an all-in-one alternative to traditional Medicare for parents needing nursing home level care
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Benefits like Old Age Pension, SNAP, and HCBS Waivers require meeting specific income, age, or care level requirements and provide ongoing financial assistance or services. Resources like SHIP (Medicare guidance) and Colorado Legal Services offer free help to most seniors regardless of income, no qualification process needed, just call for assistance.
